verses.text: Job of Uz is introduced as 'perfect and upright, one that feared God, and eschewed evil,' blessed with great family and wealth. His godliness, not his riches, is named first. God's highest commendation of a man is not his estate but a heart that fears Him and turns from evil.

Job of Uz is introduced as 'perfect and upright, one that feared God, and eschewed evil,' blessed with great family and wealth. His godliness, not his riches, is named first. God's highest commendation of a man is not his estate but a heart that fears Him and turns from evil.

Job of Uz is introduced as 'perfect and upright, one that feared God, and eschewed evil,' blessed with great family and wealth. His godliness, not his riches, is named first. God's highest commendation of a man is not his estate but a heart that fears Him and turns from evil.

When his children feast, Job rises early to offer sacrifices for each, fearing they may have sinned in their hearts. A godly father prays for souls, not just bodies. True love watches over the spiritual welfare of those it loves, and brings them continually before God.

When his children feast, Job rises early to offer sacrifices for each, fearing they may have sinned in their hearts. A godly father prays for souls, not just bodies. True love watches over the spiritual welfare of those it loves, and brings them continually before God.

Among the spirits presenting themselves before God, Satan comes, and God Himself points to Job: 'hast thou considered my servant Job?' Even the accuser acts only within God's court. The LORD is not unaware of the trials His servants face; nothing reaches them but by His leave.

Among the spirits presenting themselves before God, Satan comes, and God Himself points to Job: 'hast thou considered my servant Job?' Even the accuser acts only within God's court. The LORD is not unaware of the trials His servants face; nothing reaches them but by His leave.

Among the spirits presenting themselves before God, Satan comes, and God Himself points to Job: 'hast thou considered my servant Job?' Even the accuser acts only within God's court. The LORD is not unaware of the trials His servants face; nothing reaches them but by His leave.

Satan sneers, 'doth Job fear God for nought?' claiming Job serves only for the hedge of blessing around him; God permits the test but guards Job's life. The enemy questions whether any love God for Himself. God allows the proving, but Satan's leash is firmly in His hand.

Satan sneers, 'doth Job fear God for nought?' claiming Job serves only for the hedge of blessing around him; God permits the test but guards Job's life. The enemy questions whether any love God for Himself. God allows the proving, but Satan's leash is firmly in His hand.

In one day the Sabeans seize the oxen and donkeys and kill the servants. The first blow falls without warning. The hedge once lifted, trouble pours in, yet not a stroke comes but by the permission of God.

In one day the Sabeans seize the oxen and donkeys and kill the servants. The first blow falls without warning. The hedge once lifted, trouble pours in, yet not a stroke comes but by the permission of God.

Before that messenger finishes, another reports fire from heaven consuming the sheep, then another, the Chaldeans carrying off the camels. Blow follows blow with no pause to breathe. Affliction often crowds in, that faith may be tried to its very depth.

Before that messenger finishes, another reports fire from heaven consuming the sheep, then another, the Chaldeans carrying off the camels. Blow follows blow with no pause to breathe. Affliction often crowds in, that faith may be tried to its very depth.
Last and worst, a great wind collapses the house upon all his children, and they are dead. The dearest treasure is taken. No earthly loss cuts deeper, yet even this is within the bounds God has set.
Last and worst, a great wind collapses the house upon all his children, and they are dead. The dearest treasure is taken. No earthly loss cuts deeper, yet even this is within the bounds God has set.

Job rises, tears his robe, falls down, and worships: 'the LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD.' 'In all this Job sinned not.' Grief and worship meet in him. Faith can bless the hand that wounds, owning that all we had was always God's gift.
